findings.description: @Url parameter value flows directly to HttpUrl.resolve() without path traversal or URL override validation, allowing absolute URL override (SSRF) or path escape via ../ sequences
findings.reasoning:
The RelativeUrl.apply() method performs only a null check before passing the user-supplied URL value directly to setRelativeUrl(). In RequestBuilder.build() at line 197, this value is passed to baseUrl.resolve(relativeUrl) which follows RFC 3986 semantics. This means:
1. If the @Url value is an absolute URL (e.g., "http://evil.com/api"), HttpUrl.resolve() ignores the base URL entirely and uses the absolute URL - enabling SSRF.
2. If the @Url value contains "../" sequences (e.g., "../../admin"), they are resolved relative to the base URL path, potentially escaping the intended API path prefix.
There is no validation to ensure the resolved URL stays within the intended base URL domain/path. The setRelativeUrl() method (RequestBuilder.java:73-75) also does nothing but call toString() on the value.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: User-supplied @Url value → RelativeUrl.apply() → builder.setRelativeUrl(value) → this.relativeUrl = relativeUrl.toString() → baseUrl.resolve(relativeUrl). No sanitization or validation at any step.
evidence.notes: HttpUrl.resolve() follows RFC 3986 - absolute URLs completely override the base. '../' sequences navigate up the path hierarchy. This is a client-side library so the 'attacker' is whoever controls the @Url parameter value at runtime.

exploit_payload.scenario: An application using Retrofit passes an external/user-controlled value to a @Url-annotated parameter, allowing the attacker to redirect the HTTP request to an arbitrary URL (SSRF) or escape the intended API path via ../ sequences
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Application must pass untrusted/user-controlled input to a Retrofit @Url parameter without validating it first

flags.message: This is a client-side HTTP library where @Url is an intentional developer-controlled feature, not user input
flags.evidence: Retrofit is a type-safe HTTP client library for Android/Java. The @Url annotation is a deliberate API design allowing developers to specify dynamic endpoints. The 'attacker' would need to be the developer themselves or someone controlling the application code - this is caller responsibility by design.

flags.message: SSRF concerns in a client-side library where URL flexibility is an intentional feature misrepresent the threat model
flags.evidence: Retrofit's @Url annotation is explicitly documented to allow full URL overrides. Absolute URL override is a feature, not a bug. The library is used server-side only when the application is making outbound requests, and controlling the URL is the developer's intent. CWE-022 path traversal is also not applicable here since this is an HTTP URL, not a file path.

flags.message: The lack of URL validation is by design for this library's flexible URL parameter feature
flags.evidence: Retrofit intentionally supports absolute URL overrides via @Url to allow runtime endpoint configuration. Adding validation would break the library's documented functionality. This is not a vulnerability but an expected behavior.

sanity_check.reasoning: Retrofit is a client-side HTTP networking library where the @Url annotation is an intentional feature allowing developers to specify dynamic or absolute URLs at runtime. The 'vulnerability' described is actually the intended behavior of the library - absolute URL override is explicitly supported and documented. SSRF would only be a concern if the @Url value comes from untrusted external user input in the consuming application, which is a responsibility of the application using the library, not the library itself. CWE-022 path traversal is also misapplied here as this deals with HTTP URLs, not filesystem paths.

findings.description: @Path parameter with encoded=true allows ../ path traversal sequences and pre-encoded %2F slashes to pass through canonicalizeForPath() without sanitization, enabling URL path escape
findings.reasoning:
The addPathParam() method calls canonicalizeForPath(value, encoded) before substituting into the URL template. The PATH_SEGMENT_ALWAYS_ENCODE_SET = " \"<>^`{}|\\?#" does NOT include '.' characters.

When encoded=false (default): '/' is encoded to '%2F' and '%' is encoded to '%25', so '../' becomes '..%2F' - still potentially dangerous if the server decodes %2F.

When encoded=true (@Path(value="id", encoded=true)): '/' and '%' are NOT encoded. This means:
1. A value like '../../../admin' passes through with the '/' and '..' characters intact
2. Pre-encoded '%2F' passes through as-is (the '%' character is not re-encoded)
3. Pre-encoded '%2e%2e' or '%2E%2E' also passes through

The canonicalizeForPath() function only encodes characters in PATH_SEGMENT_ALWAYS_ENCODE_SET plus control characters. The '.' character is never in any encode set - there is no check for '..' sequences at all.

The resulting path with traversal sequences is substituted into relativeUrl which is then resolved via baseUrl.resolve(relativeUrl) in build(), navigating up the URL path hierarchy.

evidence.notes: With encoded=true: '../../../admin' passes through canonicalizeForPath unchanged since none of its characters are in the encode set. With encoded=false: '.' passes through but '/' becomes '%2F'. The concern is mainly with encoded=true mode. This is a client-side library so exploitation depends on the application passing untrusted data to @Path parameters.

exploit_payload.scenario: Application uses @Path(encoded=true) and passes user-controlled input as path parameter, allowing ../ sequences to navigate outside the intended URL path structure
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Application must use @Path(encoded=true) AND pass untrusted user input as the path parameter value

flags.message: This is a client-side HTTP library - path traversal exploitability depends entirely on caller passing untrusted data to @Path parameters
flags.evidence: Retrofit is a type-safe HTTP client library. The @Path annotation substitutes values into URL templates. The library itself cannot prevent misuse if callers pass unsanitized user input, but this is a caller responsibility pattern common in libraries.

flags.message: Path traversal (CWE-022) is typically a server-side vulnerability; this is a client-side HTTP library constructing outbound requests
flags.evidence: The finding concerns URL construction for outbound HTTP requests. While '../../../admin' could modify the intended URL path, the impact depends on server-side behavior and whether the application passes untrusted input to @Path parameters.

flags.message: The encoded=true behavior allowing unencoded slashes and percent signs is documented behavior, not a security flaw in the library itself
flags.evidence: The encoded=true parameter is explicitly designed to allow pre-encoded values to pass through. The PATH_SEGMENT_ALWAYS_ENCODE_SET intentionally excludes '.' as dots are valid URL path characters. The behavior is by design for the encoded=true use case.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code accurately reflects the described behavior - canonicalizeForPath with encoded=true does not encode '/' or '%' characters, allowing traversal sequences to pass through. However, this is a client-side HTTP library where the security responsibility lies with callers to not pass untrusted input to @Path parameters. The finding is technically accurate but its classification as CWE-022/073 is debatable since path traversal in this context affects outbound request construction rather than server-side file access, and the encoded=true behavior is intentional by design. This warrants manual review to determine if the library should add warnings or sanitization for the encoded=true case.

findings.description: addQueryParam uses OkHttp's standard HttpUrl.Builder query parameter methods - these are not path traversal sinks
findings.reasoning: The addQueryParam() method uses OkHttp's HttpUrl.Builder methods addEncodedQueryParameter() and addQueryParameter(). These are standard query parameter operations - they append to the query string, not modify the URL path. Query parameters cannot cause path traversal. OkHttp's URL builder properly handles encoding of query parameter values. The urlBuilder is initialized from baseUrl.newBuilder(relativeUrl) which validates the URL structure. This is not a path traversal vulnerability - query parameters are in a different part of the URL than the path.

evidence.notes: This is standard OkHttp API usage. Query parameters go to the query string (?name=value), not the path. Not a path traversal vulnerability.

flags.message: Query parameter operations cannot cause path traversal (CWE-073)
flags.evidence: addQueryParameter() and addEncodedQueryParameter() append to the URL query string (?name=value), not the URL path. Path traversal requires manipulation of the path component, not the query string. OkHttp's HttpUrl.Builder correctly separates these concerns.

flags.message: CWE-073 (External Control of File Name or Path) does not apply to URL query parameters
flags.evidence: CWE-073 pertains to file system path manipulation. URL query parameters are distinct from file paths and URL paths. Even if user-controlled values were passed to these methods, they would only affect the query string portion of the URL, not traverse file paths or URL path components.

sanity_check.reasoning: This is a clear false positive. The code uses OkHttp's standard HttpUrl.Builder methods to add query parameters to a URL. Query parameters (the ?key=value portion of a URL) are entirely separate from the URL path, making path traversal via these methods impossible. OkHttp properly encodes these values and the urlBuilder is initialized from a validated base URL. CWE-073 is not applicable here as this involves URL construction, not file system path operations.

findings.description: RequestBuilder.build() calls HttpUrl.resolve(relativeUrl) with no path traversal or domain validation - the relativeUrl comes directly from @Url parameter with only a null check
findings.reasoning:
This is the actual sink where the unsanitized relativeUrl is consumed. HttpUrl.resolve() implements RFC 3986 URL resolution:
1. If relativeUrl is an absolute URL (has scheme like http://, https://), it completely replaces the base URL - enabling SSRF
2. If relativeUrl starts with '//', it replaces scheme+host - enabling SSRF
3. If relativeUrl starts with '/', it replaces the path from the base URL root
4. If relativeUrl contains '../' sequences, they navigate up the path hierarchy, potentially escaping the intended base path prefix

The relativeUrl value flows from @Url-annotated parameter → RelativeUrl.apply() → setRelativeUrl() → here, with only a null check applied. No validation ensures the resolved URL stays within the intended base URL's domain or path prefix. The only defense is the null return check from resolve(), which only triggers for completely malformed URLs, not for valid but malicious URLs.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Source: @Url annotated parameter value (user-controlled string) -> ParameterHandler.RelativeUrl.apply() line 58-61 -> RequestBuilder.setRelativeUrl() line 73-75 -> RequestBuilder.relativeUrl field -> RequestBuilder.build() line 197 -> baseUrl.resolve(relativeUrl) [SINK]
evidence.notes: HttpUrl.resolve() with an absolute URL input completely ignores baseUrl. RFC 3986: if reference has a scheme component, the result is the reference. This means http://evil.com passed as @Url completely bypasses the configured base URL.

exploit_payload.scenario: Application passes user-controlled or externally-sourced string to a @Url-annotated Retrofit parameter, allowing the attacker to redirect HTTP requests to arbitrary URLs including internal services (SSRF) or escape the intended API path
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Application must pass untrusted input directly to a @Url Retrofit parameter without pre-validation

flags.message: This is a library vulnerability - exploitability depends on how callers use @Url annotation
flags.evidence: Retrofit is a HTTP client library. The @Url parameter is explicitly designed for dynamic URL specification. Whether this constitutes a vulnerability depends entirely on whether the application passes user-controlled input to @Url parameters without validation. The library itself cannot reasonably validate what URLs are 'legitimate' without knowing application context.

flags.message: The @Url feature in Retrofit is intentionally designed to allow full URL override - this is documented behavior, not a bug
flags.evidence: Retrofit's @Url annotation is explicitly documented to allow callers to specify the full URL for a request, overriding the base URL. This is by design and a core feature. Flagging this as SSRF in the library itself conflates library design with application misuse.

flags.message: The finding identifies a real risk pattern but assigns responsibility incorrectly to the library rather than to applications using it
flags.evidence: If application code passes untrusted user input directly to a Retrofit interface method annotated with @Url, SSRF can occur. However, this is an application-level vulnerability, not a Retrofit library vulnerability. The library should document the security implications, but cannot prevent misuse.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code snippet accurately reflects what is described - HttpUrl.resolve() is called with relativeUrl which can be an absolute URL, enabling SSRF if user-controlled input reaches this point. However, this is Retrofit's intentional @Url feature design, not a library bug. The real vulnerability exists at the application layer when developers pass unsanitized user input to @Url-annotated parameters. This finding correctly identifies a dangerous pattern but misattributes the vulnerability to the library rather than to application-level misuse, warranting manual review to determine if the library should add documentation warnings or validation options.

findings.description: False positive: @Url annotation in Retrofit allows full URL override - this is documented, intentional behavior, not a path traversal vulnerability
findings.reasoning:
The CodeQL queries flagged all ParameterHandler subclass apply() methods, RequestBuilder fields, and string literals related to the @Url annotation. The hypothesis is that @Url allows bypassing base URL restrictions.

However, this is false positive for these reasons:

1. The @Url annotation behavior is explicitly documented in Retrofit.java (lines 498-509): "Endpoint values may be a full URL. Values which have a host replace the host of baseUrl and values also with a scheme replace the scheme of baseUrl." This is intentional API design.

2. The relevant CWEs for this review are CWE-022/023/036/073 (path traversal). This code involves HTTP URL construction, NOT filesystem path operations. There is no file system access whatsoever in this code path.

3. setRelativeUrl() stores the value, and build() calls baseUrl.resolve(relativeUrl) - this is OkHttp's RFC 3986 compliant URL resolution, not a filesystem operation.

4. The @Url annotation is a client-side HTTP library feature. The developer writing the API interface deliberately uses @Url when they want to support dynamic URLs - this is the intended use case.

5. The ParameterHandler subclasses (Header, Path, Query, Body, Field, etc.) were also flagged but none of these involve filesystem access or path traversal vulnerabilities in the CWE-022/036 sense.

evidence.code_context: RelativeUrl.apply() -> builder.setRelativeUrl(value) -> this.relativeUrl = relativeUrl.toString() -> baseUrl.resolve(relativeUrl) in build(). All HTTP URL construction, no filesystem access.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: setRelativeUrl stores value as String, then build() uses baseUrl.resolve(relativeUrl) which is OkHttp HttpUrl resolution following RFC 3986.
evidence.notes: Retrofit documentation explicitly states: 'Endpoint values may be a full URL. Values which have a host replace the host of baseUrl and values also with a scheme replace the scheme of baseUrl.' This is documented intentional behavior. Not within scope of CWE-022/023/036/073.

flags.message: No filesystem path traversal possible - this is HTTP URL construction only
flags.evidence: The code flows through builder.setRelativeUrl() -> baseUrl.resolve(relativeUrl) using OkHttp HttpUrl RFC 3986 resolution. No filesystem access occurs at any point in the chain.

flags.message: CWE-022 (Path Traversal) does not apply to HTTP URL manipulation in a networking library
flags.evidence: CWE-022 concerns filesystem path traversal. Retrofit's @Url annotation allows HTTP endpoint URL override which is fully documented intentional behavior for a REST client library. URL host/scheme override is a feature, not a vulnerability.

flags.message: Intentional design pattern flagged as vulnerability
flags.evidence: Retrofit documentation explicitly states endpoint values may be full URLs that can override the base URL's host and scheme. This is by design for API clients that need to call multiple endpoints.

sanity_check.reasoning: This is a clear false positive. The RelativeUrl ParameterHandler in Retrofit handles HTTP URL construction using OkHttp's HttpUrl.resolve(), which follows RFC 3986 and never touches the filesystem. CWE-022 (Path Traversal) is inapplicable here as there is no file system interaction. The @Url annotation's ability to override base URLs is explicitly documented, intentional library behavior designed to support REST clients that interact with multiple endpoints or dynamic URLs.
